_known_issue: The demand and warehouse narrative paragraphs in rendered_brief are transposed. Cash figures under each heading are correct. The site reads driver copy from lib/otif.js META, never from this raw prose.
rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Scope: 140 failed orders with a combined order value of AED 1,226,642.

RANKED DRIVERS BY SHORTFALL CASH IMPACT

supplier — AED 477,794 (39% of total shortfall)
Supplier lateness clustered across the month, with late PO receipts leaving insufficient on-hand stock at pick time. In the majority of cases the delayed purchase-order quantity alone would have closed the entire unit gap had it arrived on time, confirming supplier lateness as the binding cause. A subset of these orders also showed a positive forecast error, meaning demand surge compounded the exposure, but the supplier delay was the dominant and sufficient driver in each attributed case. Immediate action: implement a purchase-order early-warning trigger so any PO tracking more than two days behind schedule automatically escalates to a dual-source or safety-stock release before the pick window opens.

warehouse — AED 313,864 (26% of total shortfall)
Forecast errors consistently underestimated actual period demand, depleting on-hand stock before replenishment could arrive. The magnitude of the miss frequently exceeded the resulting shortfall, confirming that better forecasting alone would have prevented the stock-out even without any supplier or logistics change. In a subset of these orders a late supplier PO compounded the gap, but the forecast miss was the primary driver. Immediate action: retrain the demand model on the most recent demand history and introduce a weekly bias-correction review for the highest-velocity SKUs.

demand — AED 282,823 (23% of total shortfall)
Warehouse failures split into two distinct clusters: dispatch delays where stock was physically present but held beyond the promised ship date, and genuine pick shorts where on-hand inventory exceeded the order quantity yet fewer units were picked. Both are warehouse-owned faults. Dispatch delays were the more frequent pattern; pick shorts, while fewer in count, carried material shortfall value. Immediate action: enforce a same-day dispatch cut-off audit and introduce a pick-confirmation scan that flags any picked quantity more than five percent below the system-allocated quantity before the order is closed.

logistics — AED 152,160 (12% of total shortfall)
All logistics failures were timing-only events — goods were picked in full and dispatched, but transit overages pushed delivery past the promised date. No unit shortfall was recorded in any logistics-attributed order, so the cash impact reflects order value at risk rather than lost revenue from undelivered units. In roughly half of logistics-implicated orders a warehouse dispatch delay co-occurred, but in the attributed cases the transit overage was the dominant or sole lateness driver. Immediate action: renegotiate lane SLAs with the two carriers responsible for the longest transit overages and introduce a shipment-tracking alert at the midpoint of each lane SLA.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED
The investigation could not determine whether supplier lateness was driven by a small number of repeat offenders or spread broadly across the supply base; carrier-level identity behind transit overages was not available in the signal set; and the root cause of warehouse pick shorts (system error, labour shortage, or slotting failure) could not be distinguished from the order-level data alone. These gaps should be closed before corrective actions are finalised.
